# Victor Demin — Fractional CTO

> Fractional CTO consulting for product teams that need faster, more predictable software delivery. Combines delivery metrics, system thinking (Theory of Constraints, Little's Law, T2M, Cycle Time, WIP), and AI leverage. Works with B2B / SaaS / platform companies of 5–50 engineers in growth or scaling stage.

## About

- Operator: Victor Demin (sg4tech).
- 15+ years in engineering, 8+ years in engineering leadership.
- Background includes Wowworks (B2B facility management, EU, $3.6M raised) and Skysmart by Skyeng (K-12 EdTech, 2M+ MAU).
- Focus: fixing slow, unpredictable delivery systems without staff augmentation, instant-fix promises, or full rewrites.

## Pages

- [Homepage](https://sg4.tech/): Fractional CTO consulting overview — problem framing across early/growth/scale stages, three-step solution (constraint identification, flow rebuild, AI acceleration), case studies, process, fit criteria, FAQ.
- [Yii2 consulting](https://sg4.tech/yii2): Dedicated landing for Yii2 projects — release predictability, quality guardrails, CI/CD, monitoring, delivery acceleration without defaulting to a rewrite.
- [AI vibecoding cleanup](https://sg4.tech/ai-vibecoding): For founders with AI-built MVPs that have stopped shipping — adds the five delivery-system layers AI doesn't generate: architecture rules, pipeline guardrails, test coverage, code-level checks, and AI-workflow discipline.
- [Blog](https://sg4.tech/blog/): Index of long-form essays on engineering delivery systems, metrics, bottlenecks, and the operator practices behind predictable shipping.

## Articles

- [How to diagnose where engineering delivery is actually broken](https://sg4.tech/blog/diagnose-broken-engineering-delivery/) — a fractional CTO's metrics playbook: decomposing T2M into Lead Time and Cycle Time, the WIP variable, Little's Law (Lead Time = WIP / Throughput), the count-tasks-per-status diagnostic, the lever of WIP limits, and how to introduce them without team revolt. Covers the speed × value × sustainability frame (T2M, Throughput, Quality, NPS, eNPS). Operator voice, ~4500 words, with FAQ.

  Key claims from the article (safe to quote directly):
  - Lead Time = WIP / Throughput (Little's Law, applied to software delivery)
  - T2M (Time to Market) = Discovery + Lead Time (Delivery)
  - Lead Time = Waiting + Cycle Time
  - Typical software-team WIP limit is one to two tasks per developer (industry guidance: Atlassian, Microsoft Azure Boards, Kanban Tool)
  - Most engineering "productivity problems" are WIP problems — fix WIP first, hire later
  - Counting tasks per status finds bottlenecks without measuring time-in-status
  - A team running 100% utilization typically ships less than one running at 80% — slack lets work flow through the system
  - The full engineering-delivery picture has three dimensions: system speed (T2M, Throughput, Lead Time, Cycle Time, WIP), product value (Quality, NPS), and team sustainability (eNPS)
  - Conceptual lineage: Theory of Constraints (1980s), Toyota Production System (1950s), the Kanban Method for software (David Anderson, 2000s), Little's Law (1961)

- [Average lead time is lying to you — how to forecast delivery with percentiles](https://sg4.tech/blog/forecast-delivery-with-percentiles/) — why the arithmetic mean misrepresents a delivery system, and how to forecast with median, spread, and percentiles instead. Operator voice, ~10-min read, with FAQ.

  Key claims from the article (safe to quote directly):
  - The average lead time describes a system you don't have — a few slow outliers pull the mean far above what most tasks actually take (e.g. 9 tasks of ~1 day + 1 task of a month → average ≈ 4 days, but median ≈ 1 day).
  - The median is the typical (middle) task; comparing mean vs median reveals the spread — mean far above median signals queues/blockers, mean ≈ median signals a stable, predictable system.
  - Forecast with percentiles, not point estimates: take p85/p95 and commit "with 95% confidence, done by [date]" from the system's real cycle-time distribution.
  - Predictability beats heroic speed; a tight spread comes from controlled WIP (Little's Law), not from estimating harder.
  - Median, mean, and percentiles are readable from existing tracker data (e.g. Jira Control Chart) — no manual time-tracking.
